title: Risotto agli asparagi
subtitle: Spring asparagus risotto with Parmesan and lemon
excerpt: Asparagus risotto made properly: stalks simmered into stock, tips added only in the last four minutes so they stay bright and just-tender. The result is intensely asparagus-forward rather than diluted by a generic broth. A spring dish worth the effort.
sourceNotes: Risotto agli asparagi is a northern Italian spring preparation, particularly associated with the Veneto and Lombardy where asparagus is cultivated in the Po valley. Artusi includes risotto with vegetables in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) and covers asparagus preparations in the Venetian style. The technique of simmering asparagus trimmings to make cooking stock is documented in regional northern Italian cookbooks.

content.text: The key to asparagus risotto is using the asparagus itself as stock. Snap the woody ends and cut the tips, then simmer the stalks in water for fifteen minutes. You get a pale green broth that tastes of exactly one thing. Ladle that into the rice and the asparagus flavour runs through every grain.

The tips go in only in the last four minutes. Add them earlier and they overcook into khaki mush. Add them at the right moment and they stay bright green and just-tender, giving the finished dish two textures: the creamy, starchy rice and the slightly resistant spears.

items.name: Asparagus
items.prepNote: woody ends snapped off; tips cut to 5 cm, stalks chopped

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: 20 g for cooking, 40 g cold for finishing

content.text: Snap the woody ends from the {{asparagus}} stalks where they break naturally. Cut the top 5 cm from each spear and set the tips aside. Chop the remaining stalks roughly and put in a saucepan with 1.2 litres of cold water and a pinch of salt. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes. Strain through a fine sieve, pressing the stalks. Discard the stalks. Keep the stock hot over the lowest possible heat.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and 20 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide, heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent without colouring. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look translucent at the edges and smell faintly toasted.

Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine. Stir until fully absorbed. Add the hot asparagus stock a ladleful at a time (roughly 80 to 100 ml per addition), stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to be absorbed before adding the next. After 14 minutes, taste the rice. It should be nearly done, with a faint resistance at the centre.

Add the reserved asparagus tips and stir gently. Cook for 3 to 4 more minutes, adding stock as needed, until the tips are just tender and the rice is cooked through with a slight bite. The risotto should flow slowly when you tilt the pan. Remove from the heat. Add the remaining cold butter in pieces and the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Beat vigorously for 60 seconds: this is the
. The risotto should be glossy and loose. Add the {{lemon}} zest, taste for salt, and cover for 2 minutes. Serve immediately in warm bowls.

items.fix: Remove from heat while the rice still has a faint bite; the residual heat of the mantecatura continues cooking it
items.cause: Overcooked, or too little liquid, or not enough stirring during the last few minutes

items.fix: Add the tips only when the rice is 4 minutes from done; they should be bright green at serving
items.cause: Added too early; asparagus tips need only 3 to 4 minutes
items.symptom: Asparagus tips are grey and mushy

items.fix: Season the asparagus stock well; cook the shallots fully soft before adding the rice
items.cause: Stock underseasoned, or shallots undercooked before the rice went in
items.symptom: Risotto tastes bland despite the asparagus

items.fix: Remove from heat entirely; use cold butter straight from the fridge for the mantecatura
items.cause: Pan was still over heat, or butter was not cold

content.text: Asparagus risotto is a spring dish in the Veneto and Lombardy, where asparagus has been cultivated in the river plains since at least the sixteenth century. The Bassano del Grappa variety, white and grown in the mineral-rich soil of the Veneto foothills, is the most prized in the region; green asparagus is more common elsewhere. Both work in this preparation. The dish appears in late April and is gone by June: it marks a specific moment in the seasonal calendar, and that brevity is part of what makes it worth making properly.
